content:
# Leviticus Chapter 8 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 61v–62r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 92–93

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Take Aaron and his sons with him, and the garments and the anointing oil and the bull of the sin offering and the two rams and the basket of unleavened bread,

**3** and assemble all the congregation at the entrance of the tent of meeting."

**4** And Moses did as the LORD commanded him, and the congregation was assembled at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**5** And Moses said to the congregation, "This is what the LORD has commanded to be done."

**6** Then Moses brought Aaron and his sons forward and washed them with water.

**7** And he put the tunic on him and tied the sash around him and clothed him with the robe and put the ephod on him and tied the skillfully woven band of the ephod around him, binding it to him.

**8** And he placed the breastpiece on him, and in the breastpiece he put the Urim and Thummim.

**9** And he set the turban on his head, and on the turban, in front, he set the golden plate, the holy crown, as the LORD commanded Moses.

**10** Moses then took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and all that was in it, and consecrated them.

**11** And he sprinkled some of it on the altar seven times, and anointed the altar and all its utensils and the basin and its stand, to consecrate them.

**12** And he poured some of the anointing oil on Aaron's head and anointed him, to consecrate him.

**13** And Moses brought Aaron's sons forward and clothed them with tunics and tied sashes around them and bound caps on them, as the LORD commanded Moses.

**14** Then he brought the bull of the sin offering, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on the head of the bull of the sin offering.

**15** And he slaughtered it, and Moses took the blood, and with his finger put it on the horns of the altar around about, and purified the altar, and poured out the blood at the base of the altar and consecrated it to make atonement for it.

**16** And he took all the fat that was on the entrails and the lobe of the liver and the two kidneys with their fat, and Moses burned them on the altar.

**17** But the bull and its hide and its flesh and its dung he burned up with fire outside the camp, as the LORD commanded Moses.

**18** Then he presented the ram of the burnt offering, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on the head of the ram.

**19** And he slaughtered it, and Moses threw the blood against the altar on all sides.

**20** He cut the ram into pieces, and Moses burned the head and the pieces and the fat.

**21** And he washed the entrails and the legs with water, and Moses burned the whole ram on the altar. It was a burnt offering with a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD, as the LORD commanded Moses.

**22** Then he presented the other ram, the ram of ordination, and Aaron and his sons laid their hands on the head of the ram.

**23** And he slaughtered it, and Moses took some of its blood and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot.

**24** Then he brought Aaron's sons forward, and Moses put some of the blood on the lobes of their right ears and on the thumbs of their right hands and on the big toes of their right feet, and Moses threw the rest of the blood against the altar on all sides.

**25** Then he took the fat and the fat tail and all the fat that was on the entrails and the lobe of the liver and the two kidneys with their fat and the right thigh,

**26** and from the basket of unleavened bread that was before the LORD he took one unleavened loaf and one loaf of bread with oil and one wafer, and placed them on the fat and on the right thigh.

**27** And he put all of it in the hands of Aaron and in the hands of his sons, and waved them as a wave offering before the LORD.

**28** Then Moses took them from their hands and burned them on the altar on top of the burnt offering. They were an ordination offering with a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD.

**29** And Moses took the breast and waved it as a wave offering before the LORD. It was Moses' portion of the ram of ordination, as the LORD commanded Moses.

**30** Then Moses took some of the anointing oil and some of the blood that was on the altar and sprinkled it on Aaron and his garments, and also on his sons and his sons' garments. So he consecrated Aaron and his garments, and his sons and his sons' garments with him.

**31** And Moses said to Aaron and his sons, "Boil the flesh at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and eat it there with the bread that is in the basket of ordination offerings, as I commanded, saying, 'Aaron and his sons shall eat it.'

**32** And what remains of the flesh and the bread you shall burn up with fire.

**33** And you shall not go out from the entrance of the tent of meeting for seven days, until the days of your ordination are completed, for it will take seven days to ordain you.

**34** As has been done today, the LORD has commanded to be done to make atonement for you.

**35** At the entrance of the tent of meeting you shall remain day and night for seven days, keeping the LORD's charge, so that you do not die, for so I have been commanded."

**36** And Aaron and his sons did all the things that the LORD commanded by Moses.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 61v–62r.** Both manuscript pages were examined directly. Folio 61v carries the transition from the end of chapter 7 into the opening of chapter 8. The three-column format of the Leningrad Codex is clearly visible in the photographic reproduction. The parashah break between the closing colophon of Lev 7 (vv. 37–38) and the opening of Lev 8 is visible — chapter 8 begins at what appears to be a setumah (closed section break, marked by a space within the line) rather than a petucha (open section break with a new line), reflecting the structural continuity of the priestly material. The verse numbering for chapter 8 extends fully across both folios. Folio 61v carries the opening command to Moses (vv. 1–5), the washing and vesting of Aaron (vv. 6–9), the anointing of the tabernacle and Aaron (vv. 10–13), and the sin offering bull (vv. 14–17). Folio 62r carries the burnt offering ram (vv. 18–21), the ordination ram (vv. 22–29), the sprinkling of oil and blood (v. 30), and the seven-day ordination instructions (vv. 31–36). The Masoretic pointing throughout is characteristic of the Tiberian tradition, and no obvious scribal corrections or erasures are visible in the photographic facsimile.

Structurally, chapter 8 is the narrative fulfillment of the instructions given in Exodus 28–29. The Leningrad Codex preserves the chapter as a single cohesive narrative unit — notably, there is no major parashah break within the chapter, underscoring that the ordination ceremony is narrated as a single unified action under Moses' sole priestly agency. The repeated formula כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe — "as the LORD commanded Moses") appears at vv. 4, 9, 13, 17, 21, 29, and 36, functioning as a structural refrain that punctuates the narrative into its component ritual units.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 92–93 (LXX, pa_0096–pa_0097).** Both Vaticanus pages were examined directly. The chapter marker Γ CAP. XXXV is visible at the top of page 92 (pa_0096), marking the LXX's chapter enumeration — which diverges from the standard Hebrew chapter numbering in places. The three-column uncial hand is legible throughout both pages. The LXX text of Leviticus 8 (= LXX Lev 8, corresponding to MT Lev 8) runs continuously across both pages. Page 93 (pa_0097) carries the chapter marker Γ CAP. XXXVI at the top, indicating the chapter break into what corresponds to MT Leviticus 9.

The uncial script shows no visible lacunae or corrections on these pages. The LXX text of Leviticus 8 is in general close agreement with the MT, with the expected terminological divergences in the cultic vocabulary (τελείωσις for milu'im, ἐπίθεμα for tenufah) and some syntactic expansion in a few verses. Notable: the Vaticanus text at vv. 10–12 expands the anointing account slightly, and the phrase corresponding to MT's "as the LORD commanded Moses" (v. 36) appears in the LXX as καθότι συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ ("just as the LORD directed/ordered Moses"), using συντάσσω ("to direct, to command in order") for the standard tsivvah.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"The anointing oil" / shemen ha-mishchah (vv. 2, 10, 12, 30).** Hebrew שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה (shemen ha-mishchah — "the oil of the anointing / the anointing oil"). As in Exodus 29 and 30:22–33, shemen ha-mishchah is the specially compounded oil of Exodus 30:22–33, made with myrrh, cinnamon, aromatic cane, cassia, and olive oil. It is not a generic oil but the singular sacred oil manufactured once for the consecration of the tabernacle and the Aaronic priesthood. "The anointing oil" renders shemen ha-mishchah precisely and is the consistent rendering throughout this project. The LXX renders τὸ ἔλαιον τοῦ χρίσματος ("the oil of the anointing / chrism"), in close agreement. The article in both Hebrew (ha-mishchah) and Greek (τοῦ χρίσματος) is definite, pointing to this specific, well-known, previously defined oil.

2. **"The Urim and Thummim" / urim ve-tummim (v. 8).** Hebrew הָאוּרִים וְהַתֻּמִּים (ha-urim ve-ha-tummim). The Urim and Thummim are objects of uncertain nature placed inside the breastpiece (choshen) of the high priest, used for oracular discernment of the divine will (cf. Num 27:21; 1 Sam 28:6; Ezra 2:63). Their physical form is not described in the Pentateuch; they may have been stones, lots, or inscribed tablets. The names likely derive from roots meaning "lights" (or possibly "curses") and "perfections" (or "innocences"), but etymological certainty is elusive. Per project convention, "Urim and Thummim" are preserved untranslated as technical terms, with the definite article rendered in English as "the Urim and Thummim." The LXX renders τὴν δήλωσιν καὶ τὴν ἀλήθειαν ("revelation/declaration and truth"), providing a functional interpretation: the Urim gives revelation and the Thummim gives truth. The LXX's interpretive rendering is noted but this translation follows the established convention of preserving the Hebrew terms untranslated.

3. **"The holy crown" / nezer ha-qodesh (v. 9).** Hebrew נֵזֶר הַקֹּדֶשׁ (nezer ha-qodesh — "the consecrated/holy crown / the diadem of holiness"). The nezer (crown, diadem, consecration) is the golden plate (tzitz) affixed to the front of the high priest's turban, engraved with the words קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה (qodesh la-YHWH — "Holy to the LORD," Exod 28:36). The root נזר (nazar) means to dedicate, to consecrate, to separate; the nezer is the visible emblem of the high priest's separation unto holiness. "The holy crown" (ESV, KJV) renders nezer ha-qodesh by emphasizing both the royal/diademic character and the holiness-dedication. NASB: "the holy diadem." The LXX renders τὸ πέταλον τὸ χρυσοῦν τὸ ἁγίασμα τὸ ἅγιον ("the golden plate, the holy consecration / the holy sanctified object"), a more expansive rendering. This translation follows the MT's concision: "the holy crown."

4. **"Consecrated them" / vayqaddesh otam (v. 10).** Hebrew וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֹתָם (vayyqaddesh otam — "and he consecrated them / and he made them holy"). The verb qadash (in the piel/intensive stem) means to consecrate, to set apart as holy, to make holy. In vv. 10–12 Moses performs three distinct acts of consecration via the anointing oil: (a) the tabernacle and all its furnishings (v. 10), (b) the altar specifically, with seven spinklings (v. 11), and (c) Aaron himself (v. 12). The three-stage consecration pattern moves from the outer structure inward to the high priest who will serve within it. "Consecrated" (piel of qadash) is the established formal rendering throughout the series. The LXX renders ἡγίασεν ("made holy / sanctified"), using the verb ἁγιάζω, in close agreement.

5. **"Purified the altar" / vaykhatter et-ha-mizbeach (v. 15).** Hebrew וַיְחַטֵּא אֶת-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (vayykhatte' et-ha-mizbeach — "and he decontaminated / purified the altar"). The verb חטא in the piel (khitte') is the denominative causative of chattat (sin offering): it means "to perform a sin-offering procedure upon, to decontaminate, to purify from sin-contamination." This is the technical terminology of priestly purification ritual. When blood is applied to the altar horns (v. 15a), the altar is thereby "decontaminated" or "purified" — its accumulated ritual impurity is removed. "Purified" renders vayykhatte' accurately in context. Some translations use "cleansed" (NASB) or "consecrated" (KJV), but "purified" (ESV) best captures the decontamination/purification sense of the piel. The LXX renders ἡγνίσατο ("he purified / made ritually clean"), using ἁγνίζω ("to purify, to make clean"), in close agreement.

6. **"The ram of ordination" / eil ha-millu'im (v. 22).** Hebrew אֵיל הַמִּלֻּאִים (eil ha-millu'im — "the ram of the fillings / the ram of ordination"). The noun מִלֻּאִים (milu'im) derives from the root מָלֵא (male', "to fill") and refers to the "filling of the hands" (mille' yad) — the ceremony of investiture by which the priests receive their office. The idiom "fill the hands" for priestly installation appears throughout the Pentateuch (Exod 28:41; 29:9; Lev 8:33; cf. 16:32; Num 3:3). The "filling" may refer to placing the portions of the sacrifice into the hands of the priest-designate (as in vv. 27–28), symbolically investing him with priestly prerogative. "Ram of ordination" (ESV, NASB) renders eil ha-millu'im by its functional meaning in the institutional context. This translation uses "ordination" consistently. The LXX renders τὸν κριὸν τὸν τῆς τελειώσεως ("the ram of the ordination / completion"), using τελείωσις ("completion, perfection, ordination") for milu'im, as established in the Lev 7 colophon variant note.

7. **"The lobe of the right ear / the thumb of the right hand / the big toe of the right foot" (vv. 23–24).** Hebrew תְּנוּךְ אֹזֶן אַהֲרֹן הַיְמָנִית ... בֹּהֶן יָדוֹ הַיְמָנִית ... בֹּהֶן רַגְלוֹ הַיְמָנִית (tenukh ozen Aharon ha-yemanit ... bohen yado ha-yemanit ... bohen raglo ha-yemanit). The word תְּנוּךְ (tenukh) occurs only here and in the parallel text of the leper's purification (Lev 14:14, 17, 25, 28) in the entire Hebrew Bible — it means the "lobe" or "cartilage tip" of the ear. The word בֹּהֶן (bohen) means the "thumb" (of the hand) or "big toe" (of the foot). The three-point blood application — ear, thumb, toe — consecrates the whole person synecdochically: hearing (the word of God), working (with the hands in service), and walking (in the way of the LORD). This is a total-person consecration rite. The three-point pattern was borrowed from the Day of Atonement rite and applied in the leper's purification ceremony (Lev 14), suggesting the ordination ceremony was a paradigm for other purification rites. "Lobe of the right ear / thumb of the right hand / big toe of the right foot" renders each term precisely. The LXX renders τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ ὠτὸς τοῦ δεξιοῦ ("the tip/extremity of the right ear"), τὸ ἄκρον τῆς χειρὸς τῆς δεξιᾶς ("the tip/extremity of the right hand"), and τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ ποδὸς τοῦ δεξιοῦ ("the tip/extremity of the right foot"), using ἄκρον ("tip, extremity, end") for both tenukh and bohen — generalizing the anatomical specifics but preserving the three-point pattern and the right-side emphasis.

8. **"Wave offering" / tenufah (vv. 27, 29).** Hebrew תְּנוּפָה (tenufah). As noted in the chapter 7 variant discussion, tenufah designates the ritual gesture of waving the offering before the LORD. In vv. 27–29 Moses places the ordination offerings in the hands of Aaron and his sons and waves them — then takes them back and burns them on the altar. This "wave and return" procedure is notable: the offerings are first waved (presented before the LORD in the wave gesture) and then burned. Moses himself waves the breast (v. 29) and retains it as his own portion, as Moses acts as the officiating priest throughout the ordination ceremony. "Wave offering" renders tenufah consistently with the chapter 7 rendering. The LXX renders ἀφελὼν ... ἐπέθηκεν ἐπίθεμα ("having taken them ... placed them as an offering placed before"), using ἐπίθεμα for tenufah — the standard LXX rendering that emphasizes placement rather than waving motion.

9. **"Moses' portion" (v. 29).** Hebrew לְמֹשֶׁה הָיָה לְמָנָה (le-Moshe hayah le-manah — "it was for Moses as a portion / as a share"). The manah (מָנָה) is a portion, an allotted share. This verse makes explicit what is otherwise unstated: Moses acts as the officiating priest throughout Leviticus 8, and the wave breast of the ordination ram is his priestly portion — the standard wave breast portion of the peace offering that belonged to the presiding priest (cf. Lev 7:31). Moses is the one human being in the Pentateuch who serves as priest before Aaron is installed; after ch. 8 Moses steps back and the Aaronic priesthood takes over (Lev 9). "Moses' portion" renders le-Moshe ... le-manah idiomatically. The LXX renders τῇ μερίδι Μωυσῇ ἐγενήθη ("it became the portion of Moses"), in close agreement.

10. **"The days of your ordination" / yemei milu'eikhem (v. 33).** Hebrew יְמֵי מִלֻּאֵיכֶם (yemei milu'eikhem — "the days of your fillings / the days of your ordination"). Yemei milu'im ("the days of ordination") is the technical term for the seven-day period during which Aaron and his sons remain at the entrance of the tent of meeting, confined to the sacred precincts as part of their installation. The seven days correspond to the seven days of creation — a structural parallel that undergirds the theology of the tabernacle as a new creation. "The days of your ordination" renders yemei milu'eikhem accurately and idiomatically. The LXX renders ἡμέρας τελειώσεως ὑμῶν ("the days of your perfection/completion"), using τελείωσις consistently for milu'im.

11. **"Keeping the LORD's charge" / ushemartem et-mishmeret YHWH (v. 35).** Hebrew וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת-מִשְׁמֶרֶת יְהוָה (u-shemartem et-mishmeret YHWH — "and you shall keep the charge/watch/duty of the LORD"). The phrase mishmeret YHWH (the charge of the LORD) is a significant cultic-priestly formula: the priests' fundamental obligation is to "keep the charge" — to guard, to maintain, to watch over — the sacred sphere of YHWH's presence. The root שָׁמַר (shamar) means to keep, guard, observe, maintain. The mishmeret is the body of priestly obligations and restrictions. "Keeping the LORD's charge" (ESV, NASB: "keep the charge of the LORD") renders u-shemartem et-mishmeret YHWH accurately. The LXX renders φυλάξεσθε τὰ φυλάγματα κυρίου ("you shall guard/keep the ordinances/obligations of the LORD"), using the cognate pair φυλάσσω / φύλαγμα ("to guard/keep" / "what is kept/guarded") for shamar / mishmeret — in close structural agreement.

12. **"So that you do not die" (v. 35).** Hebrew לְבִלְתִּי תָמוּתוּ (le-bilti tamuttu — "lest you die / in order that you do not die"). This warning connects directly to the priestly theology of sacred boundaries: improper approach to the holy will result in death. The seven-day confinement is not merely ceremonial — it is a life-and-death boundary condition. The same warning appears in Lev 10:9 (in the context of the prohibition of alcohol before service) and elsewhere in priestly legislation. The ordination period must be completed in full, without deviation, or death results. "So that you do not die" renders le-bilti tamuttu accurately. The LXX renders ἵνα μὴ ἀποθάνητε ("in order that you do not die"), in direct agreement.

13. **Compliance formula — "as the LORD commanded Moses" (vv. 4, 9, 13, 17, 21, 29, 36).** Hebrew כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe — "as the LORD commanded Moses"). This structural refrain appears seven times in chapter 8, one for each major ritual action. The sevenfold repetition corresponds to the seven-day ordination period and functions to authorize each ritual action by divine mandate. The formula is rendered consistently throughout as "as the LORD commanded Moses." At v. 36, the formula is reversed to apply to Aaron and his sons: "Aaron and his sons did all the things that the LORD commanded by Moses" (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH be-yad Moshe — "as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses"), where the instrument is "by the hand of Moses" (be-yad Moshe) rather than the simple direct object. "By Moses" renders be-yad Moshe (lit. "by the hand of Moses") idiomatically; some translations render "through Moses." The distinction is slight: be-yad ("by the hand of") is the standard idiom for prophetic/mediatorial transmission of divine commands. "By Moses" is chosen as more natural English. The LXX renders at v. 36 καθότι συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ ("just as the LORD directed Moses"), using συντάσσω for tsivvah.

14. **"Fire offering" / isheh (vv. 21, 28).** Hebrew אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה (isheh la-YHWH — "a fire offering to the LORD"). As established from Lev 1, isheh ("fire offering") designates any offering that is consumed by fire on the altar. In vv. 21 and 28 the formula "a pleasing aroma, a fire offering to the LORD" (re'ach nichoach, isheh la-YHWH) appears as the liturgical characterization of both the burnt offering ram and the ordination ram. "Fire offering" renders isheh consistently. The LXX renders εἰς ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας, κάρπωμα κυρίῳ ("for a pleasant aroma, an offering burned/fruit-offering to the LORD"), using κάρπωμα ("a thing burned as fruit/offering, holocaust-portion") for isheh. Some LXX manuscripts use ὁλοκαύτωμα ("whole burnt offering") here, but Vaticanus uses κάρπωμα — the more exact rendering of the fire-offering category distinct from the total holocaust.

15. **"A pleasing aroma" / re'ach nichoach (vv. 21, 28).** Hebrew רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach — "a smell of soothing/resting / a pleasing aroma"). This formulaic phrase has been rendered consistently throughout the series as "a pleasing aroma." The phrase describes the ascending smoke of the sacrifice as an aroma that is pleasing, soothing, or restful to the LORD — an anthropomorphic description of divine reception of the sacrifice. The root נוח (nuach) means to rest, to settle, to be at peace. "A pleasing aroma" (ESV, NASB, KJV: "sweet savor" in older tradition) renders re'ach nichoach accurately. The LXX renders εἰς ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας ("for a pleasant/fragrant smell"), in close agreement.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 92–93, pa_0096–pa_0097) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8:2 | shemen ha-mishchah ("the anointing oil") | τὸ ἔλαιον τοῦ χρίσματος ("the oil of the anointing/chrism") | Standard agreement. LXX uses χρῖσμα ("anointing/chrism") for mishchah consistently. |
| 8:2 | ve-et shenei ha-eilim ("and the two rams") | καὶ τοὺς δύο κριούς ("and the two rams") | Direct agreement. |
| 8:8 | ha-urim ve-ha-tummim ("the Urim and Thummim") | τὴν δήλωσιν καὶ τὴν ἀλήθειαν ("the revelation/declaration and the truth") | Notable interpretive divergence. The LXX provides a functional translation — "revelation" (δήλωσις) for Urim and "truth" (ἀλήθεια) for Thummim — rather than preserving the untranslated Hebrew terms. The LXX's rendering reflects an interpretation of Urim as related to אוֹר ("light/illumination → revelation") and Thummim as related to תֹּם ("completeness/integrity/truth"). This translation follows the project convention of preserving "Urim and Thummim" untranslated. |
| 8:9 | nezer ha-qodesh ("the holy crown") | τὸ πέταλον τὸ χρυσοῦν τὸ ἁγίασμα τὸ ἅγιον ("the golden plate, the holy consecration") | LXX expands significantly, specifying τὸ πέταλον τὸ χρυσοῦν ("the golden plate") — drawn from the Exodus 28:36 context — rather than translating nezer directly. The LXX does not use a word for "crown" or "diadem" for nezer here; τὸ ἁγίασμα τὸ ἅγιον ("the holy thing set apart as holy") interprets the consecration-function of the nezer. The MT's nezer as "crown/diadem" preserves more clearly the royal-priestly significance of the emblem. |
| 8:10 | vayyqaddesh otam ("and he consecrated them") | ἡγίασεν αὐτά ("he made them holy") | Standard agreement: LXX ἁγιάζω for Hebrew qadash (piel). |
| 8:11 | vayyazzeh mimmennu al-ha-mizbeach sheva pe'amim ("and he sprinkled from it on the altar seven times") | καὶ ἔρρανεν ἀπ' αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὸ θυσιαστήριον ἑπτάκις ("and he sprinkled from it upon the altar seven times") | Full agreement. The sevenfold sprinkling of oil on the altar (v. 11) is distinct from the blood sprinkling of the sin offering (v. 15). LXX renders ῥαίνω for Hebrew naza (sprinkle). |
| 8:12 | vayyitssoq mi-shemen ha-mishchah al-rosh Aharon ("and he poured some of the anointing oil on Aaron's head") | καὶ ἐπέχεεν ἀπὸ τοῦ ἐλαίου τοῦ χρίσματος ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν Ααρων ("and he poured from the oil of the anointing upon the head of Aaron") | Full agreement. LXX uses ἐπιχέω ("to pour upon") for the Hebrew natsaq / yatsaq ("to pour"), which is more precisely a "pouring out/over" verb than a sprinkling verb — the LXX captures the pouring nature of the initial anointing accurately. |
| 8:15 | vayykhatte' et-ha-mizbeach ("and he purified/decontaminated the altar") | ἡγνίσατο τὸ θυσιαστήριον ("he purified the altar") | LXX renders the piel of חטא (khitte') with ἁγνίζω ("to purify, to make ritually clean"). The MT's piel of chattat carries the specific nuance of decontamination-via-sin-offering procedure; the LXX's ἁγνίζω is a broader purification term that does not specifically reference the sin offering procedure. The MT's meaning is somewhat more technically specific. |
| 8:15 | vayyishpokh et-ha-dam el-yesod ha-mizbeach ("and he poured out the blood at the base of the altar") | καὶ τὸ αἷμα ἐξέχεεν πρὸς τὴν βάσιν τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου ("and the blood he poured out at the base of the altar") | Full agreement. |
| 8:22 | eil ha-millu'im ("the ram of ordination") | τὸν κριὸν τὸν τῆς τελειώσεως ("the ram of the ordination/completion/perfection") | Standard LXX rendering: τελείωσις for milu'im. Established convention; see Lev 7:37 variant note. |
| 8:23 | tenukh ozen Aharon ha-yemanit ("the lobe of Aaron's right ear") | τὸ ἄκρον τοῦ ὠτὸς τοῦ δεξιοῦ Ααρων ("the tip/extremity of Aaron's right ear") | LXX renders tenukh (lobe/cartilage tip of the ear) with ἄκρον ("tip, extremity, end"), a general term. The MT's tenukh is more anatomically specific (cartilage lobe) but ἄκρον captures the tip/extremity correctly. The three-part rite is structurally preserved in LXX. |
| 8:23 | bohen yado ha-yemanit ("the thumb of his right hand") | τὸ ἄκρον τῆς χειρὸς τῆς δεξιᾶς ("the tip/extremity of the right hand") | LXX renders bohen ("thumb/big toe") with ἄκρον ("tip/extremity"), as at v. 23a. LXX omits the specific identification of the thumb vs. big toe, using the general "tip of the right hand / right foot" language. The MT's bohen is anatomically precise: the thumb (bohen yad) and big toe (bohen regel) are the extremities that are explicitly named. |
| 8:26 | chalat lechem shemen achat ve-chalat matsah achat ve-reqiq echad ("one loaf of bread with oil and one unleavened loaf and one wafer") | ἄρτον ἐξ ἐλαίου ἕνα καὶ ἄρτον ἐξ ἄρτων ἀζύμων ἕνα καὶ λάγανον ἓν ("one bread from oil and one bread from unleavened breads and one flat cake") | LXX agreement on three types of bread drawn from the ordination basket. Minor variation in the specific Hebrew-to-Greek correspondence for the bread types: the LXX renders the three bread types with ἄρτον ἐξ ἐλαίου ("oil bread"), ἄρτον ἐξ ἄρτων ἀζύμων ("bread of unleavened breads"), and λάγανον ("flat cake/wafer"). The MT has chalat lechem shemen (a bread-loaf of oil), chalat matsah (an unleavened loaf), and reqiq (a wafer). The LXX's λάγανον is the standard LXX term for reqiq ("thin wafer/flat cake"). |
| 8:27 | vayyasem et-ha-kol al kapei Aharon ("and he placed all of it on the palms of Aaron") | καὶ ἐπέθηκεν τὰ πάντα ἐπὶ τὰς χεῖρας Ααρων ("and he placed all things upon the hands of Aaron") | LXX renders kappayim ("palms/hands") with χεῖρας ("hands"), which is the standard Greek equivalence. The MT's kappayim (dual of kaph — "palm, hollow of the hand") is slightly more specific than the general word for hands, but χεῖρας captures the placing-in-hands gesture accurately. |
| 8:27 | vayyanef otam tenufah ("and he waved them as a wave offering") | καὶ ἀπήνεγκεν ἐπίθεμα ἔναντι κυρίου ("and he brought/carried it as a placed-offering before the LORD") | Notable divergence. The MT's vayyanef (from נוּף nuf — "to wave, to swing, to move back and forth") describes the specific waving gesture of the tenufah ritual. The LXX renders with ἀπήνεγκεν ἐπίθεμα ("brought/carried something placed"), using ἐπίθεμα ("something placed on/before") for tenufah — as consistently elsewhere in Leviticus. The LXX's rendering emphasizes the presentational aspect (bringing before the LORD) rather than the waving motion. |
| 8:28 | millu'im hem ("they are ordination offerings") | τελείωσίς ἐστιν ("it is an ordination/completion") | LXX renders the plural מִלֻּאִים (milu'im, "ordination offerings") with the singular τελείωσις, treating the ordination ceremony as a single conceptual event rather than a plurality of offerings. Minor structural divergence. |
| 8:29 | le-Moshe hayah le-manah ("it was for Moses as a portion") | τῇ μερίδι Μωυσῇ ἐγενήθη ("it became the portion of Moses") | Full structural agreement. The LXX explicitly identifies this as Moses' priestly portion (μερίς), preserving the significance of Moses' unique priestly role in the ordination ceremony. |
| 8:33 | yemei milu'eikhem ("the days of your ordination") | ἡμέρας τελειώσεως ὑμῶν ("the days of your completion/perfection") | Standard LXX rendering of milu'im as τελείωσις. See vv. 22, 28 above. |
| 8:33 | shiv'at yamim yemalle' et-yedekhem ("for seven days he will fill your hands") | ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας τελειώσει τὰς χεῖρας ὑμῶν ("for seven days he will perfect/complete your hands") | The MT's idiomatic yemalle' et-yedekhem (lit. "he will fill your hands" — the technical formula for priestly investiture) is rendered by the LXX as τελειώσει τὰς χεῖρας ("will complete/perfect your hands") — the LXX's τελείωσις language for milu'im is here applied verbally. The MT's "filling of the hands" idiom is more evocative of the physical act of placing offerings in the priest's hands (as in vv. 27–28); the LXX's "perfecting/completing the hands" is more metaphorical. Both point to the same investiture reality. |
| 8:35 | mishmeret YHWH ("the charge of the LORD") | τὰ φυλάγματα κυρίου ("the charges/observances of the LORD") | LXX uses τὸ φύλαγμα (plural: τὰ φυλάγματα — "the guarded things, the things to be kept, the obligations to be observed") for mishmeret. The LXX's plural emphasizes the multiplicity of priestly obligations, while the MT's singular mishmeret treats the priestly charge as a unified concept. |
| 8:36 | ka-asher tsivvah YHWH be-yad Moshe ("as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses") | καθότι συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ ("just as the LORD directed/commanded Moses") | LXX uses συντάσσω ("to direct in order, to arrange, to command") for tsivvah ("to command"), and renders be-yad Moshe ("by the hand of Moses") simply as τῷ Μωυσῇ (dative: "to Moses"), omitting the "hand of" idiom. The MT's be-yad ("by the hand of") is a standard idiom for mediated command — God commands by/through Moses' hand. The LXX's rendering is a simplification that preserves the meaning but loses the mediation-of-agency idiom. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 8)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה (shemen ha-mishchah) | the anointing oil | The specially compounded sacred oil; LXX: τὸ ἔλαιον τοῦ χρίσματος |
| אוּרִים וְתֻמִּים (urim ve-tummim) | the Urim and Thummim | Preserved untranslated per project convention; LXX: τὴν δήλωσιν καὶ τὴν ἀλήθειαν ("revelation and truth") |
| נֵזֶר הַקֹּדֶשׁ (nezer ha-qodesh) | the holy crown | The golden plate on the turban; emblem of high-priestly consecration; LXX: τὸ πέταλον τὸ χρυσοῦν τὸ ἁγίασμα τὸ ἅγιον |
| מִלֻּאִים (milu'im) | ordination (offerings / period) | From male' ("to fill"); "filling of the hands" = investiture; LXX: τελείωσις ("completion/perfection") |
| אֵיל הַמִּלֻּאִים (eil ha-millu'im) | the ram of ordination | The specific sacrificial ram of the installation ceremony |
| תְּנוּכָה (tenukh) | lobe | The cartilage lobe of the ear; anatomically specific; LXX: ἄκρον ("tip/extremity") |
| בֹּהֶן (bohen) | thumb / big toe | The thumb (bohen yad) and big toe (bohen regel); LXX: ἄκρον ("tip/extremity") |
| כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe) | as the LORD commanded Moses | Structural refrain; seven occurrences in Lev 8; LXX: καθότι συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ |
| מִשְׁמֶרֶת יְהוָה (mishmeret YHWH) | the LORD's charge | The body of priestly obligations; shamar = "to keep/guard"; LXX: τὰ φυλάγματα κυρίου |
| וַיְקַדֵּשׁ (vayyqaddesh, piel) | consecrated | To make holy, to set apart; LXX: ἡγίασεν |
| וַיְחַטֵּא (vayykhatte', piel) | purified | Decontamination via sin-offering procedure; LXX: ἡγνίσατο |
| רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) | a pleasing aroma | Formulaic description of divine acceptance of sacrifice; LXX: εἰς ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Any altar-consumed offering; LXX Vaticanus: κάρπωμα |
| תְּנוּפָה (tenufah) | wave offering | Ritual presentation gesture; LXX: ἐπίθεμα ("placed offering") |
| מָנָה (manah) | portion | The priest's allotted share; LXX: μερίς |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Whole-burnt altar offering; established from Lev 1 |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established from Lev 4; LXX: περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας |
| אַהֲרֹן (Aharon) | Aaron | English name form throughout |
| מֹשֶׁה (Moshe) | Moses | English name form throughout |
| יִשְׂרָאֵל (Yisrael) | Israel | English name form throughout |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) | tent of meeting | Established convention |
| מִשְׁכָּן (mishkan) | tabernacle | The dwelling-place; distinct from ohel mo'ed (the outer tent structure) |
| קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim) | most holy | Supreme sacral category; LXX: ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων |

— LOGOS
